Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Bertrand Russell

1872 – 1970

I will treat “Russell” as Bertrand Russell.

I will treat “Russell” as Bertrand Russell. The view that emerges from his mature work is not a simple materialism, not Cartesian dualism, not idealism, and not a theory of an immaterial soul. It is best reconstructed as an event-based neutral monism: the world is made of events or particulars that are not intrinsically divided into “mental” and “material”; minds and bodies are different causal organizations of this same neutral material; consciousness is not the essence of mind but a late, complex, intentional function; the soul is not a substance but a historically and bodily organized series of events; and theology matters because doctrines of soul, immortality, free will, fear, consolation, and value are among the chief pressures that make the mind-body problem seem metaphysically urgent.

1. The controlling thesis: Russell’s philosophy of mind is an anti-substance theory of mind

Russell’s mature position can be stated in one sentence: there is no fundamental metaphysical divide between soul and body, mind and matter, or consciousness and physical reality; there are only events arranged under different systems of causal relations. In The Analysis of Mind, Russell says explicitly that he wants to reconcile the “materialistic tendency” of psychology with the “anti-materialistic tendency” of physics, and he does so by taking over from William James and the American new realists the idea that the world’s ultimate stuff is “neither mental nor material,” but more primitive than either category. He also announces that he wants to reject the claim that consciousness is the essence of mind.

This is a decisive break with the theological-Cartesian inheritance. The older picture says: there is a body in space and time, and a soul or mind in time but not space; the soul is the seat of consciousness, freedom, and immortality; matter is extended, mind is private, and the two need some special connection. Russell’s answer is not that the soul is really matter in the old billiard-ball sense. Rather, he denies both poles of the inherited opposition. In his 1928 essay “What Is the Soul?” he says modern physics has undermined matter as a solid ultimate thing, while modern psychology has undermined mind as a substantial entity; what is needed is not mind as the basis of body or body as the basis of mind, but something “neither mind nor body” from which both are constructed.

So Russell’s philosophy of mind is not “materialism” in the crude sense. He rejects the old metaphysical substance called matter. But he is also hostile to the theological inference that if matter is not ultimate, the soul must be. His most characteristic move is this: both matter and mind are logical or causal constructions out of events. The event in the head that we call a percept may belong simultaneously to the physical history of a brain and to the mental history of a person. The same item can be placed in two explanatory systems.

That is the central doctrine from which his philosophy of consciousness, selfhood, memory, desire, belief, will, religion, and immortality follows.

2. Development of the view: from acquaintance to neutral monism

Russell’s philosophy of mind changes substantially between the early analytic period and his mature neutral-monist period. In The Problems of Philosophy in 1912, he is still working with a fairly traditional epistemological contrast between immediately known sense-data and inferred physical objects. He holds that we are directly acquainted with sense-data, with some mental events by introspection, with memory contents, and probably with the self, while physical objects and other minds are known by description rather than direct acquaintance.

This early epistemology is not yet the full mature ontology. In 1912, Russell still gives introspection and self-acquaintance a place that his later work will qualify or dissolve. He is already suspicious of naïve realism about ordinary physical objects, but he has not yet fully abandoned the act-object picture of consciousness. The early view still permits something like: “I, the subject, am acquainted with my sense-data and my mental acts.” In later Russell, the subject becomes less and less a primitive ingredient and more and more a construction out of connected experiences, memories, habits, and bodily relations.

The next important step is Russell’s 1914 essay “The Relation of Sense-data to Physics.” There he argues that physics must be empirically verifiable, but the only things directly observed are sense-data. His problem is therefore to show how physical objects can be constructed, or at least correlated, with the sensible data on which empirical science depends. Crucially, he does not treat sense-data as mental in the idealist sense. He introduces “sensibilia,” possible objects of sensation that have the same metaphysical and physical status as actual sense-data but are not necessarily data for any mind.

This is already a proto-neutral-monist move. Sense-data are not inner mental pictures sealed inside a soul. They are parts of the actual subject matter of physics. Subjectivity, for Russell, is not an occult mental property but a physiological and causal feature: a datum is “private” because it is tied to the body and perspective of a perceiver, not because it belongs to a separate mental substance.

Russell later takes the decisive step. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that he coined the expression “neutral monism” in 1914 and then accepted the doctrine around 1919, developing it in The Analysis of Mind and The Analysis of Matter. The mature doctrine is that both minds and material objects are constructed from neutral events or particulars, and that the difference between mental and physical is not a difference in ultimate stuff but in organization, causal law, and explanatory role.

By The Analysis of Matter in 1927, Russell’s view becomes more sophisticated. Physics, he argues, gives us abstract structure, causal order, and mathematical relations, but not the intrinsic character of the events that instantiate that structure. Psychology gives us better access to at least some intrinsic characters, namely percepts. The “physical” and the “mental” are therefore not two realms but two abstract ways of organizing and knowing events.

This later doctrine has become important in contemporary philosophy under the name Russellian monism. Contemporary Russellian monists use Russell’s point that physics describes structure rather than intrinsic nature to argue that consciousness might reveal, or at least be related to, the intrinsic nature of the physical world. But Russell himself should not be hastily assimilated either to panpsychism or to reductive physicalism. His position is closer to neutral monism: mind and matter are constructions from a more basic event-world.

3. The metaphysics: events first, mind and matter later

Russell’s mature ontology has three levels.

At the first level are events, particulars, or neutral stuff. These are not mental substances, not material substances, and not souls. They are the basic items out of which both minds and material objects are constructed. Russell’s language varies across works, but the metaphysical tendency is stable: substance disappears, and the world becomes a network of events ordered by relations.

At the second level are physical objects. A physical object is not an unknowable substance hidden behind appearances. It is a system of events, appearances, or particulars grouped by physical causal laws. In The Analysis of Mind, Russell says a material object is not a substance but a system of particulars analogous to sensations and sometimes including sensations. In The Analysis of Matter, he extends this by saying that electrons, protons, and points are logical structures composed of events.

At the third level are minds. A mind is also not a substance. It is a system of events grouped by psychological causal laws, especially memory, habit, association, expectation, imagery, desire, and what Russell calls “mnemic causation.” Mental life consists centrally of sensations and images, but these are not united by an immaterial ego. They are united by causal, mnemonic, and bodily relations.

This gives Russell a distinctive answer to the mind-body problem: the problem is misframed if we begin with two substances and then ask how they interact. There are not two substances. There are events. Some events are grouped in the way physics groups them; some are grouped in the way psychology groups them; and some, especially perceptual events in the brain, belong to both groupings.

This also explains why Russell is not a behaviorist in the crude sense. He is interested in behavior, habit, and bodily causation, but he does not reduce mind to publicly observable behavior alone. Percepts and images matter. The intrinsic quality of experience matters. But he refuses to infer from this that there must be an inner soul-substance. The mental is real, but it is not substantial.

4. Physics, psychology, and the “intrinsic character” problem

Russell’s mature philosophy of mind cannot be understood without his philosophy of physics. His central claim is that physics gives only a structural, mathematical, causal description of the world. It tells us how events are related, how they change, and how they can be mathematically ordered, but not what they are intrinsically. This is one of the deepest points in his later thought.

Perception, by contrast, gives us acquaintance with at least some intrinsic character. When one sees a color, hears a sound, or feels pain, one is not merely grasping abstract structure. One is acquainted with a qualitative occurrence. Russell therefore thinks psychology has a special role: it supplies some knowledge of the intrinsic nature of events, while physics supplies the abstract causal skeleton.

This does not mean psychology replaces physics. Russell explicitly denies that psychology is an autonomous spiritual science floating free of bodily causation. He says physics is causally dominant in the sense that brain-events are strongly connected with mental events, and that the physical causal order is indispensable. But physics alone does not give the full intrinsic character of the events it structurally describes.

The result is a subtle position. Against old materialism, Russell says matter is not a hard, self-sufficient substance whose intrinsic nature physics has fully revealed. Against idealism, he says not everything is mental, and percepts are only one part of the physical world. Against dualism, he says the mental and the physical are not two kinds of stuff. Against eliminativism, he says perceptual qualities are genuine constituents of the world, not illusions to be eliminated.

This is why contemporary philosophers connect Russell to Russellian monism. Modern Russellian monism often argues that consciousness may be related to the intrinsic properties that physics leaves undescribed. Some versions become panpsychist; some become panprotopsychist; some remain neutral-monist. Russell’s own position is best read as neutral-monist rather than straightforwardly panpsychist: he does not say every event is conscious, but he does deny that the ultimate constituents are intrinsically “material” in the old sense.

5. Consciousness: not the essence of mind

Russell’s most radical psychological thesis is that consciousness is not the essence of mind. He explicitly rejects the idea that what makes mental events mental is that they are conscious. He follows William James’s attack on “consciousness” as a separate entity: thoughts, sensations, images, beliefs, and memories exist, but “consciousness” is not an additional substance or act hovering over them.

This is not the same as denying experience. Russell does not deny that there are sensations, images, pains, colors, and perceptual qualities. What he denies is that there is an extra metaphysical ingredient called consciousness that must be added to these events to make them mental. The “consciousness” doctrine, in his view, is often the ghost of the old soul doctrine: the scholastic soul is refined into the Cartesian ego, then into the transcendental subject, then into pure consciousness. Russell tries to stop this progression by denying that the subject or consciousness is a primitive metaphysical item.

In The Analysis of Mind, he proposes that consciousness is complex and derivative. A sensation may be conscious, but it need not be. There may also be unconscious beliefs and desires. Consciousness, when it occurs, involves reference to an object: it must be consciousness “of” something. Russell analyzes this “ofness” through images, belief, memory, and objective reference. A conscious state is not merely a raw occurrence; it is an occurrence functioning in a system of meaning.

This is one of the most important places where Russell’s view differs from many later uses of “consciousness.” Contemporary philosophers often distinguish phenomenal consciousness, access consciousness, self-consciousness, and intentionality. Russell’s official analysis of consciousness sometimes looks closer to access-consciousness or intentional awareness than to raw phenomenal feel. He does preserve raw qualitative occurrence under the headings of sensation, percept, or intrinsic character, but he does not make “consciousness” itself the name of an irreducible phenomenal glow.

The consequence is that mind becomes a matter of degree. Russell says explicitly that consciousness is complex and far from universal, and that mind itself is a matter of degree. More primitive organisms may have sensations, habits, or mnemic causal patterns without the full structure of reflective consciousness. Mentality increases with the complexity of memory, habit, imagery, anticipation, and belief.

A rigorous extraction of Russell’s philosophy of consciousness should therefore distinguish four layers: first, neutral events; second, perceptual or sensory events with qualitative character; third, mnemic and imaginal organization; fourth, consciousness proper as object-directed, meaning-involving awareness. Russell is not always terminologically consistent, but this layered reconstruction makes his scattered claims coherent.

6. Sensation and perception: against the inner-theater model

Russell’s theory of perception begins from the fact that ordinary objects are not immediately given in the way common sense supposes. In early works, he says we are acquainted with sense-data and know physical objects by description. A table, for example, is not immediately known as a physical object; what is immediately given are visual and tactile data, from which the physical table is inferred or constructed.

But Russell does not conclude that sense-data are private mental pictures inside the soul. In his 1914 work, he insists that sense-data are not mental in the sense required by idealism. They are part of the actual subject matter of physics. Their privacy derives from bodily perspective and physiological causation, not from their being locked inside a non-spatial mind.

This has a major implication for hallucination and illusion. Abnormal sense-data are not intrinsically less real than normal sense-data. They differ by their correlations and causal connections. A hallucinated object is not unreal because the sensory event is a ghostly mental item; rather, it fails to fit into the stable public causal system by which ordinary physical objects are constructed.

Perception, for Russell, is not pure sensation. It is sensation plus interpretation, memory, images, expectation, and belief. The perceptual object as we ordinarily understand it is a cognitive achievement built out of simpler events. This makes perception fallible without making sensation unreal.

This is also why other minds are not immediately given. In his early epistemology, Russell says we know other minds through bodies and sense-data, not by direct acquaintance. In the mature view, the same point is reframed: other persons are systems of events inferred through bodily, behavioral, linguistic, and causal patterns. There is no direct vision of another soul-substance.

7. Images, memory, and mnemic causation

Russell’s mature psychology is built around the distinction between sensations and images. Sensations are tied to present bodily and environmental causation. Images are causally connected with previous sensations and are central to memory, imagination, expectation, and thought. The mind is not a container that holds mental objects; it is a causal system in which present events are shaped by past events.

The key concept is mnemic causation. Russell uses this to describe cases where an organism’s present response depends on its past history. Habit, memory, learned association, and intelligent response are all mnemic phenomena. A burnt child fears the fire not merely because of present stimulation, but because of a retained effect of past experience.

This concept does much of the work that the soul traditionally did. The soul was supposed to explain personal continuity, memory, disposition, character, and purposive action. Russell replaces that explanatory role with mnemic causal organization. A person persists not because an immaterial subject remains numerically identical through time, but because there is a connected causal history of memory, habit, bodily continuity, and character formation.

This also explains why Russell treats mind as graded. A system becomes more mind-like as its present responses are increasingly structured by past experience, images, habits, expectations, and beliefs. There is no need for a metaphysical threshold at which a soul suddenly enters matter.

8. Belief, meaning, and intentionality

Russell’s theory of belief is central because it connects consciousness to objective reference. A belief is not a simple inner glow. It has content; it is about something; it can be true or false. In The Analysis of Mind, Russell says knowing is an external and complicated relation, and that a belief’s objective reference comes from meaning. The content of belief is complex and may consist of words, images, sensations, or combinations of these.

This is essential for his treatment of religious belief. Russell’s example is direct: belief in God means, at minimum, belief that God exists. A religious belief is not merely a feeling, posture, or inner state; insofar as it is belief, it makes a claim about reality. That claim requires evidence.

Russell therefore separates two things that religious traditions often combine: the emotional or existential value of religious experience, and the truth of religious propositions. He can respect the former while rejecting the latter when unsupported. This distinction appears clearly in Religion and Science, where he says mystical emotion may have great value if freed from unwarranted beliefs, but that there is no method of factual truth except science.

The theory of belief also explains Russell’s suspicion of wishful thinking. In The Analysis of Mind, he treats some religious believing as a “desire-for-belief”: where facts are difficult, frightening, or unavailable, human beings may desire not just objects but beliefs themselves. This is not a casual polemic; it is part of his psychology of desire and cognition.

9. Desire: behavior, discomfort, and unconscious motivation

Russell rejects the simple introspective theory that desire is always a conscious state directed at a known object. He argues that much desire is unconscious or only retrospectively interpreted. Psychoanalysis, in his view, strengthens the case that desire is not always transparent to the subject. An outside observer may sometimes understand a person’s desires better than the person does.

Russell’s account of desire is behavioral and causal, but not crudely behaviorist. Desire is associated with a cycle of restlessness, activity, discomfort, and eventual quiescence or satisfaction. Conscious desire arises when the person has a true belief about what state of affairs will bring satisfaction. Primitive desire, however, need not involve such belief.

This matters for theology because Russell thinks many religious beliefs are psychologically intelligible as desire-shaped beliefs. Belief in God can express a desire for cosmic protection, moral order, or parental safety; belief in immortality can express fear of death and longing for continuity. The point is not that every religious belief is psychologically reducible to fear. The point is that desire can generate or sustain belief where evidence is weak.

This is also why Russell’s philosophy of religion belongs inside his philosophy of mind. His critique of religion is not merely external; it depends on his analysis of belief, desire, fear, consolation, and the human need to project value onto the universe.

10. Emotion and the body

Russell’s treatment of emotion is strongly embodied. He is sympathetic to the James-Lange approach, according to which emotions are closely tied to bodily changes as felt. He treats emotions as complexes built from sensations, images, and physiological processes.

This fits his anti-soul psychology. An emotion is not a movement of an immaterial soul. It is a bodily-psychological event-complex: muscular tension, visceral change, sensation, imagery, memory, interpretation, and action-tendency. The mental and the bodily are not two sealed regions; emotional life is one of the clearest cases in which their distinction becomes artificial.

Theologically, this matters because religious experience is often emotional: awe, fear, reverence, dependence, guilt, hope, ecstasy, resignation. Russell does not have to deny these emotions to reject the metaphysical doctrines attached to them. His position allows religious emotion to be real, powerful, and sometimes valuable, while denying that it proves God, soul, or immortality.

11. Will, agency, and freedom

Russell rejects the idea of will as a special, irreducible mental faculty. In The Analysis of Mind, he argues that voluntary action does not require a new ingredient called volition in addition to sensations, images, desires, beliefs, and causal laws. What is called will is analyzable through ordinary psychological mechanisms, including desire, motor imagery, bodily habit, and expectation.

In Religion and Science, he connects this directly with theology. The doctrines of sin, responsibility, and free will depend historically on the idea that the soul can initiate action in a way not governed by natural causation. Russell thinks this is indefensible. Decisions result from desires and causal antecedents; an uncaused act of volition is not an explanation but a metaphysical fiction.

This does not mean that Russell denies all practical distinctions between voluntary and involuntary action. His point is subtler: the distinction can be made within psychology and physiology, without appealing to a soul. An action may be called voluntary when it arises through certain desire-belief-image mechanisms rather than reflex or external compulsion. But there is no need for a contra-causal will.

The theological implications are large. If the will is not an uncaused faculty of the soul, then sin, guilt, merit, punishment, and salvation cannot be grounded in the traditional metaphysics of an autonomous spiritual agent. Russell’s ethics and politics are consequently more therapeutic, educational, and social than punitive or supernatural.

12. The self: from probable acquaintance to logical construction

Russell’s early view allowed that we are probably acquainted with the self, though he already found the question difficult. In The Problems of Philosophy, he distinguishes awareness of mental events from awareness of a subject that has them, and he is cautious about whether the self is directly known.

His mature view is much more anti-substantialist. The self is not an ingredient inside each thought. Russell criticizes theories that treat every mental act as involving a subject in addition to the object and act, suggesting that this “subject” is a remnant of the old soul. The person is better understood as constituted by relations among thoughts, memories, bodily events, and causal histories.

This does not abolish persons in ordinary life. Russell is not saying there are no people. He is saying that a person is not metaphysically simple. A person is a series, system, or organization of events connected by memory, habit, bodily continuity, and social recognition. The self is real in the way a nation, a river, or a biological organism is real: not as an indivisible substance, but as an organized historical process.

This is the exact point at which the doctrine of the soul loses its metaphysical foundation. If the self is an organized causal history rather than a simple substance, then personal survival after death requires the survival of the relevant organization: memories, habits, dispositions, character, and experiential continuity. A bare surviving “soul” with no such continuity would not be the person in any meaningful sense.

13. The soul: Russell’s direct answer

Russell’s explicit answer to “What is the soul?” is: not a simple substance, not a separately existing immaterial thing, and not an entity for which modern science supplies evidence. In his 1928 essay, he says science gives no indication of the existence of the soul or mind as an entity. What we call mind and matter are convenient ways of grouping events.

He also says that mind, matter, electrons, protons, and souls are best treated as “logical fictions” or histories of events. This must be read carefully. Russell does not mean that thoughts, pains, memories, or bodies are unreal. He means that the supposed underlying substances are not primitive entities. The reality lies in events and their organization, not in a hidden bearer behind them.

The soul doctrine, for Russell, is historically bound to a substance metaphysics inherited from Aristotle, scholasticism, grammar, and theology. In Religion and Science, he says the old notion of substance was partly derived from subject-predicate grammar: because sentences have subjects and predicates, philosophers imagined things must contain enduring subjects with qualities attached. Once substance is abandoned, the traditional soul loses its philosophical support.

But Russell is not merely deflating a word. He is giving a replacement theory. The explanatory functions once assigned to soul are redistributed: memory is handled by mnemic causation; agency by desire, belief, and habit; consciousness by object-directed cognitive organization; personality by bodily and mnemonic continuity; moral aspiration by human ideals; religious consolation by psychology. The soul is not replaced by nothing. It is replaced by a complex naturalistic theory of persons.

14. Immortality: not metaphysically impossible, but scientifically unsupported

Russell’s argument against personal immortality is probabilistic and scientific, not a dogmatic metaphysical proof. This is important. In What I Believe, he says God and immortality find no support in science, and that physiological evidence strongly suggests thinking depends on brain structure. But he also says psychical research could in principle supply evidence for survival if the evidence became strong enough. It would be unscientific, on his own view, to reject overwhelming evidence merely because it was unwelcome.

This is a more careful position than simple atheistic dismissal. Russell’s claim is that, given what we know about memory, personality, and brain dependence, personal survival is highly improbable. If personality depends on memories and habits, and if these depend on bodily and neural organization, then the decay of the body removes the known basis of personal continuity.

His rejection of immortality follows from his theory of personal identity. If a person is a connected organization of mental and bodily events, then survival requires continuation of that organization. A disembodied soul that lacks memory, character, and habits is not meaningfully the same person. Conversely, if some future science showed that those structures continued after bodily death, Russell’s general method would require considering the evidence.

Russell therefore combines metaphysical anti-dualism with empirical openness. He does not prove immortality impossible from first principles. He argues that the soul-substance theory is obsolete and that the empirical evidence points toward extinction.

15. God, religion, and the psychology of consolation

Russell’s theological remarks should not be treated as digressions. They are a sustained application of his philosophy of mind. He asks: why do human beings believe in God, immortality, cosmic purpose, sin, and providence? His answer involves desire, fear, habit, inherited doctrine, and the need to humanize an indifferent universe.

In What I Believe, he says belief in God and immortality lacks scientific support. He does not claim that all conceivable God-hypotheses are logically refuted; rather, he says no such hypothesis has enough probability on the evidence available. He treats personal immortality as more empirically testable than many forms of theism, because survival claims could in principle be supported by evidence about post-mortem memory or communication.

In “Why I Am Not a Christian,” he attacks familiar arguments such as the first-cause argument and emphasizes the role of education, fear, and the desire for safety in religious belief. The point is psychological as well as logical: religious belief often persists not because its arguments are strong but because it answers emotional needs.

In Religion and Science, Russell gives his most balanced formulation. He rejects religious doctrines when they purport to describe facts without evidence, but he allows that mystical emotion may have great value once separated from unwarranted belief. This is an important distinction: Russell does not deny awe, reverence, moral seriousness, or contemplative experience. He denies that such experiences constitute a method of factual knowledge about supernatural beings.

This yields a disciplined view of theology: religious emotions are data for psychology and ethics; religious propositions are claims requiring evidence; religious institutions are social phenomena; religious metaphysics often preserves obsolete theories of substance, soul, and will.

16. “A Free Man’s Worship”: the existential side of Russell’s philosophy of mind

Russell’s early essay “A Free Man’s Worship” gives the existential counterpart to his anti-soul metaphysics. The essay is earlier than his mature neutral monism, and Russell later noted that its theoretical ethics did not exactly match his later view, especially concerning the objectivity of good and evil. But he still regarded its general attitude as valid for those without dogmatic religious beliefs.

The essay’s core thought is that modern science discloses a world without built-in human purpose, without guaranteed cosmic justice, and without personal survival after death. Human ideals must therefore be created, cherished, and lived within those limits rather than projected onto the universe as divine facts.

This is not nihilism. Russell’s point is that value does not become worthless because it is not cosmically guaranteed. Love, knowledge, courage, beauty, and freedom remain human goods. What must be renounced is the demand that the universe itself validate them. The mind achieves dignity not by discovering that nature secretly shares our ideals, but by refusing to surrender its ideals to brute fact.

This is why the theological dimension is so central. Russell’s rejection of the soul does not merely answer a metaphysical question; it changes the ethical posture of human life. Without immortality, persons are finite. Without providence, values are not cosmic decrees. Without an immaterial will, moral life must be understood causally and socially. Without a divine mind behind nature, meaning becomes a human achievement rather than a metaphysical inheritance.

17. Russell’s philosophy of mind reconstructed as a systematic doctrine

A comprehensive Russellian philosophy of mind can be extracted as the following interconnected theses.

First, the anti-substance thesis: neither matter nor soul is an ultimate substance. The traditional soul is an inheritance from obsolete metaphysics, and matter in the old sense has also been dissolved by modern physics.

Second, the neutral-base thesis: the fundamental constituents are events or particulars that are not intrinsically divided into mental and physical. The mental and the physical are later classifications.

Third, the logical-construction thesis: minds, bodies, physical objects, electrons, selves, and souls are not primitive metaphysical simples; they are constructions, histories, or organizations of events.

Fourth, the causal-organization thesis: the difference between mind and matter lies chiefly in causal grouping. Physics groups events by physical causal laws; psychology groups events by mnemic, associative, imaginal, and behavioral laws.

Fifth, the embodiment thesis: mental events are not outside space in a spiritual realm. Conscious mental states are connected with events in the head, and percepts can be parts of both the physical world and a person’s mental history.

Sixth, the mnemic thesis: memory, habit, and learned response are central to mentality. The mind is a historically organized system, not a static container or immaterial subject.

Seventh, the derivative-consciousness thesis: consciousness is not the essence of mind. It is a complex function involving object-reference, imagery, belief, and meaning. Sensations, desires, and beliefs may occur without full consciousness.

Eighth, the intentionality thesis: belief and consciousness involve objective reference. A belief is about something and can be true or false; religious beliefs are therefore not exempt from evidential assessment merely because they are emotionally important.

Ninth, the anti-volitional-substance thesis: will is not a supernatural faculty. Voluntary action is analyzable through desires, beliefs, images, bodily habits, and causal laws.

Tenth, the personal-continuity thesis: a person is a series or organized history of events connected by memory, habit, body, and character. This makes personal immortality empirically unlikely unless those continuities can survive bodily death.

Eleventh, the epistemic-humility thesis: science gives structural and causal knowledge; perception gives some acquaintance with intrinsic character; religious and metaphysical claims must be judged by evidence and probability.

Twelfth, the ethical-existential thesis: the collapse of soul, providence, and cosmic purpose does not abolish value. It relocates value in human life, imagination, love, knowledge, and courage.

Together, these theses form a coherent Russellian philosophy of mind: neutral in ontology, causal in explanation, embodied in psychology, anti-substantialist about selfhood, skeptical about immortality, and humanistic about value.

18. Non-English reception: what appears outside the Anglo-American frame

A brief multilingual survey confirms that non-English discussions often emphasize Russell’s neutral monism as the key to his mind-body theory, rather than treating his work only as analytic epistemology. In French reception, François Loth presents neutral monism as a direct challenge to the need for a bridge between the physical and the mental, stressing Russell’s idea that mental and physical items differ by context or disposition rather than intrinsic property.

A French article in Methodos similarly reads Russell’s neutral monism as an attempt to reintroduce the body into philosophy of mind: sensation belongs both to the mental and physical orders, and the mental/physical distinction is not intrinsic but organizational. It also notes a tension: The Analysis of Mind seeks reconciliation between materialist psychology and anti-materialist physics, while The Analysis of Matter still leaves room for inference from brain states to mental states.

Spanish-language sources also frame Russell through “monismo neutral” and the mind-brain problem. Spanish search results and academic listings identify Russell, James, and Mach as major figures in neutral monism, and one Spanish article explicitly discusses the mind-brain problem through Russell’s “double-aspect” or neutral-monist theory.

A Spanish entry at filosofia.org places Russell’s treatment of religion and God alongside his treatment of mind and matter, describing him as theoretically agnostic but practically atheist, and noting that the central axis of his philosophy is often organized around the traditional ideas of mind and matter without simply dividing reality into two domains.

German-language reception, at least in broad reference form, also emphasizes “neutraler Monismus”: the view that mind and matter are reducible to identical neutral elements, associated with Mach, James, and Russell, with Russell credited for the term.

This multilingual reception is useful because it resists a narrow picture of Russell as merely a logician or epistemologist. Outside the narrow Anglo-American analytic canon, the mind-body and religion-science dimensions of Russell often remain visibly connected: soul, body, matter, mind, God, and immortality are treated as part of one problem-complex.

19. Tensions and unresolved problems in Russell’s view

Russell’s position is powerful, but not without tensions.

The first tension concerns the neutrality of the neutral stuff. If the basic events include percepts with qualitative character, are they really neutral, or are they proto-mental? If the basic events are known mainly through physics, are they really neutral, or are they proto-physical? Contemporary Russellian monism inherits this difficulty: some versions become panpsychist, some panprotopsychist, some neutral-monist, and some structural realist.

The second tension concerns consciousness. Russell rejects consciousness as an entity and treats it as derivative, but he also wants perceptual quality to reveal intrinsic character. This creates a delicate balance. If phenomenal qualities are central to our knowledge of intrinsic character, then consciousness-like phenomena seem philosophically important after all. Russell can answer by distinguishing raw perceptual quality from reflective consciousness, but he does not always keep the terminology clean.

The third tension concerns the self. Early Russell allows probable acquaintance with the self; later Russell treats the self as a construction. This is not necessarily contradiction, because he may have changed his mind, but it does mean one must not read The Problems of Philosophy as if it already contained the full mature doctrine.

The fourth tension concerns other minds. Russell can explain other persons as inferred systems of events, but this risks making interpersonal knowledge too indirect. His own framework has resources for addressing this through behavior, language, bodily expression, and causal continuity, but the problem of other minds remains a pressure point.

The fifth tension concerns theology and value. Russell wants to reject religious metaphysics while preserving human value, awe, and moral seriousness. This is philosophically coherent, but emotionally demanding. “A Free Man’s Worship” gives a heroic answer; later Russell becomes less confident about objective good and evil, though he retains the general anti-dogmatic attitude.

The sixth tension concerns freedom and responsibility. Russell’s causal psychology undermines contra-causal free will, but practical ethics still needs distinctions among compulsion, deliberation, education, responsibility, and reform. Russell’s naturalistic framework can draw these distinctions, but it cannot ground them in the old theology of the freely choosing soul.

20. Final synthesis

Russell’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a sustained dismantling of the soul-body picture and a reconstruction of mental life without metaphysical dualism. He does not begin with an immaterial soul and ask how it knows matter. He does not begin with solid matter and ask how it produces ghostly consciousness. He begins with events.

From those events, physical objects are constructed by the causal and mathematical methods of physics. Minds are constructed by mnemic, imaginal, emotional, linguistic, and behavioral organization. Consciousness is not the essence of mind but an advanced function of object-directed awareness. The self is not a substance but a history. The soul is not an entity but a misleading name for an organized pattern of events. Immortality is not logically impossible, but it lacks scientific support because the known conditions of personality are bodily and mnemonic. God is not disproved as a bare logical possibility, but religious claims lack evidential standing unless they can meet ordinary standards of belief. Religious emotions may remain humanly valuable, but they do not authorize supernatural metaphysics.

The result is austere but not shallow. Russell’s view is not a reduction of persons to dead matter; it is a rejection of the idea that either “dead matter” or “immortal soul” names the ultimate furniture of the world. The ultimate furniture is event-like, relational, and only partly known. Human beings are finite organizations of such events, capable of knowledge, love, fear, illusion, courage, and value. That is the philosophy of mind that can be extracted from Russell: a neutral-monist, event-structural, embodied, anti-substantialist, anti-theological but ethically serious theory of mind and personhood.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
